1: nefesh ki techeta (a soul/person who sins): the subject of Lev 5 is the individual in specific sinful situations. The four cases in vv. 1-4 all begin with nefesh (soul/person) as the moral agent. veshama qol alah (heard the voice of an oath/curse): the witness who heard the public adjuration to testify but remained silent. venasa avono (he bears his iniquity/guilt): the expression nasa avon (to bear iniquity) is the standard formula for unabsolved moral burden — guilt that remains on the person until resolved. Its opposite is nasa avon in the sense of ‘forgive’ (God bearing/taking away the iniquity, e.g., Exod 34:7). The guilt of the silent witness: failure to speak up in a legal process is a sin requiring atonement.
2: asher yiga bechol davar tamei (who touches any unclean thing): the touchability of impurity is a core Levitical axiom — uncleanness transfers by contact. venelam mimenu (and it was hidden from him): inadvertent contact is still real contact; the uncleanness accrues even without awareness. vehu tamei ve-ashem (and he is unclean and guilty, verb asham): the verb asham (to be/become guilty, to incur guilt) is distinct from the noun asham (guilt offering). Both appear in this chapter: the verb describes the state; the noun describes the remedy. The Hebrew legal system takes unconscious infraction seriously — objective guilt accumulates even when subjective awareness is absent.
3: tumat adam (human uncleanness): the category of impurity arising from contact with human bodily states (discharge, skin disease, corpse). venelam mimenu (was hidden from him): the second occurrence of the ‘hidden/unaware’ qualifier. The repeated emphasis on inadvertent impurity in vv. 2-4 establishes an important theological principle: the gap between what we know and what is actually true about our moral state. The guilt offering (asham) provides a remedy precisely for this gap — sin whose existence one may not even know about.
4: ki yishava letabei bifei (who swears rashly with his lips): the adjective bitui (rash utterance) describes an impulsive, unconsidered oath. lehara o leheitiv (to do evil or to do good): the oath may have been to do something harmful or something beneficial — what makes it a sin is its rashness and subsequent neglect. venelam mimenu (was hidden from him): the same formula for the third time, here in the context of forgotten oaths. The principle: a vow taken and not kept, even if forgotten, creates a real moral obligation that requires resolution. The NT counterpart: James 5:12, ‘let your yes be yes and your no be no.’
5: vehitvadah asher chata aleha (he shall confess the sin he has committed): the verb hitvadah (Hitpael of yadah) is the technical term for liturgical confession. This is the first step in the atonement process — oral acknowledgment of specific sin precedes the sacrificial offering. The sequence established here (awareness → confession → sacrifice → atonement) is the fundamental structure of OT repentance. In the NT, 1 John 1:9 (‘if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins’) preserves the same structure, with Christ's sacrifice as the permanent ground of atonement.
6: vehevi et ashamo laYHWH (he shall bring his guilt offering to YHWH): the Hebrew noun asham appears here as the technical offering-type for these cases. There is a complexity in Lev 5 that has puzzled translators: vv. 1-13 use both chattat (sin offering) and asham (guilt offering) apparently interchangeably (v.6 calls it both asham and chattat). The scholarly consensus is that in this section the two terms overlap; the stricter distinction (asham for violations of property rights or sacred objects, chattat for impurity and inadvertent sin) is clearer in chs 4-5:13. nikapper alav hakohen mechatatoh (the priest makes atonement for his sin): the verb kipper (Piel) is the atonement-making act. The full range of kipper covers: ‘to cover,’ ‘to wipe clean,’ ‘to ransom.’ All three nuances apply to what Christ's sacrifice accomplishes.
7: ve'im lo tashig yado dei seh (if his hand cannot reach [the value of] a sheep): the idiomatic lo tashig yado (his hand does not reach) is the Levitical expression for financial inability. The graduated sacrifice system in vv. 7-13 is theologically significant: YHWH adjusts the atonement mechanism to the offerer's means. A poor person's turtledoves are as effective as a wealthy person's lamb; a destitute person's flour is as effective as the doves. Atonement is not a privilege of the wealthy. The same principle governs the NT: Christ's sacrifice is equally available to all, regardless of economic status (Gal 3:28; Rev 22:17).
8: veqarav el hakohen (he shall bring it to the priest): the priest as mediator is essential — the offerer cannot self-administer atonement. vemalaq et rosho mikol erpo (he wrings its head from its neck without completely severing it): the specific slaughter method for birds differs from four-footed animals (which are slit at the throat). The wringing separates without severing — a detail prescribed and specific. The precision of the sacrificial instructions communicates that YHWH's atonement mechanism is exactly specified, not approximated.
9: vehizah middam hachatat al qir hamizbeach (he sprinkles some of the blood of the sin offering on the side of the altar): blood manipulation is the critical act in all sacrifice. The blood goes to the altar — the divine ‘address’ — not merely discarded. vehanish'ar badam yimatzei el yesod hamizbeach chattat hu (the remainder of the blood is drained at the base — it is a sin offering): blood at the base of the altar is the prescribed disposal. The blood declares the offering's nature: the animal's life poured out in the place of the offerer's life.
10: veet hasheni ya'aseh olah kamishpat (the second bird he offers as a burnt offering according to the prescribed procedure): the two-bird offering for the poor: one chattat (sin offering) for the atonement function, one olah (burnt offering) for the dedication/consecration function. The pairing reflects the dual dimension of restored covenant relationship: sin dealt with (chattat) + self re-presented to YHWH (olah). venislach lo (and he is forgiven): the declarative formula of forgiveness — the sacrificial outcome is stated as completed, not hoped-for. The same finality characterizes NT forgiveness through Christ (Heb 10:14).
11: ve'im lo tasig yado lishnei torim (if his hand cannot afford even two turtledoves): the second concession. asirit ha-ephah solet (a tenth of an ephah of fine flour): approximately 2.3 liters of flour — the minimum possible offering. lo yasim aleiha shemen (he must not put oil on it): the flour offering for the poor is stripped of oil (the characteristic of the grain offering, minchah) to signal that it is not a minchah but functions as a chattat. ve-lo yiten aleiha levonah (nor shall he add frankincense): similarly stripped of incense. The deliberate differentiation signals that the medium is fully interchangeable with blood for the purpose of atonement when poverty demands it — while maintaining the theological distinctions of the system.
12: veqamatz hakohen mimenu melo qumtzo (the priest takes from it a handful, his fistful): the kometz (handful, the amount a fist can grasp) is the priest's act that transforms the grain into a sacrifice. azkaratah (its memorial portion, azkarah): the portion burned on the altar — the noun derives from zakar (to remember/mention). The azkarah is the portion that ‘mentions’ the offerer before YHWH — the God-ward dimension of the sacrifice. The concept of memorial/remembrance before God is central to the Passover (Exod 12:14: zikkaron, memorial), and the Lord's Supper is explicitly a memorial (‘do this in remembrance of me,’ 1 Cor 11:24-25).
13: vekipper alav hakohen al chatato (the priest makes atonement for him for his sin): identical outcome formula as v.6 — the flour-atonement is fully effective. vehayeta lakohen kaminchah (it shall belong to the priest, as with the grain offering): the remainder of the flour sacrifice goes to the priests — the economy of the temple system sustains the priestly ministry from the substance of the people's atonement offerings. The principle recurs throughout Lev 6-7 in the detailed priestly portions.
14: A new divine speech formula: vayedaber YHWH el Moshe lemor (YHWH spoke to Moses, saying) — marking the transition to a distinct section. Lev 5:14-19 introduces the asham proper: the guilt/reparation offering for trespass against holy things (me'ilah, sacred encroachment). The previous section (vv. 1-13) used ‘asham’ loosely for various sin offerings; here the technical distinction begins: the asham is specifically for violations that require reparation in addition to atonement.
15: nefesh ki tim'ol ma'al (a soul who commits a trespass/act of unfaithfulness): the verb ma'al (to trespass, commit sacrilege, act treacherously) carries a connotation of covenant violation beyond mere error. It appears in contexts of violation of sacred trust: Numbers 5:6 (unfaithfulness against YHWH), Joshua 7:1 (Achan's ma'al), Ezek 18:24 (apostasy). bequdshei YHWH (against YHWH's holy things): misappropriation of consecrated property — using for secular purposes what belongs to YHWH. ve-asham (he becomes guilty): the state before offering. ayil tamim (a flawless ram): the asham requires a ram — a more substantial animal than the lamb/goat of the chattat, reflecting the reparative dimension of this offering.
16: ve-et asher chata min haqodesh yeshalem (he shall make restitution for what he sinned against the holy thing): the asham is unique among offerings in requiring both the sacrificial act AND financial restitution. vachamishito yosif alav (and shall add a fifth to it): a 20% surcharge on the value of the misappropriated sacred property. The combination of sacrifice + restitution + penalty communicates: atonement is not merely an internal religious act but includes concrete redress for objective harm. The NT counterpart: Zacchaeus's fourfold restitution (Luke 19:8) illustrates the same principle — genuine repentance makes restitution where possible.
17: ve'im nefesh ki techeta ve-ashetah achat mikol mitzvot YHWH asher lo te'asenah veloh yada (if a soul sins by doing any one of the things YHWH has commanded not to be done, though he did not know it): this is the most expansive case — sin against any divine commandment, even without awareness. ve-asham venasa avono (and he becomes guilty and bears his iniquity): the guilt is objective and real regardless of subjective knowledge. This verse establishes the principle of objective guilt: the moral order has an objective reality that conscience may not track. Romans 5:12-14 expresses the NT version: sin reigned even before the law was given explicitly, because the objective moral order existed before its codification.
18: vehevi ayil tamim min hatzon (he shall bring a flawless ram from the flock): the asham for unknown violation of a commandment requires the same ram as for known misappropriation of sacred things (v.15). The equal atonement for known and unknown sin reflects a consistent theology: the remedy is determined by the nature of the offence (violation of the divine order), not by the quality of the offender's awareness. al shiggatoh asher lo yada (for the unintentional error he did not know): shegagah (error, inadvertence) is the technical term for unintentional sin throughout the sacrificial system — it contrasts with be-yad ramah (with a high/defiant hand, Num 15:30), for which no sacrifice is prescribed.
19: asham hu asham asham laYHWH (it is a guilt offering — he has truly incurred guilt before YHWH): the emphatic declaration closes the asham section of ch5. The repetition (asham asham) is emphatic: this is genuinely an asham-case, a real guilt incurred before YHWH. The emphatic language addresses a potential objection: how can one be truly guilty for something one did not know? Answer: guilt before YHWH is defined by objective violation of the divine order, not by subjective awareness. The remedy is therefore also objective: the asham ram's blood provides real, not merely symbolic, atonement for real, not merely felt, guilt.
